content:
# Exodus Chapter 28 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~48v-50r (PDF pp. 96-99); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 70-71 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_028_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_028_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_028_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_028_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_028_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE PRIESTLY VESTMENTS:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 28 across three folio pages in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter transitions from sacred architecture to sacred personnel -- from the tabernacle and its furnishings (chapters 25-27) to the priests who will serve within it. The chapter opens by setting apart Aaron and his sons (vv. 1-5), then details the priestly garments in descending order of complexity: the ephod (vv. 6-14), the breastpiece of judgment with its twelve stones and the Urim and Thummim (vv. 15-30), the robe of the ephod (vv. 31-35), the gold plate inscribed "Holy to YHWH" (vv. 36-38), the tunic, turban, and sash (v. 39), garments for Aaron's sons (vv. 40-41), and the linen undergarments (vv. 42-43). The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 70-71) presents the LXX text with a substantially different arrangement, reordering sections and adding material not found in the MT, particularly in the breastpiece section.

---

## The Appointment of the Priests (vv. 1-5)

**28:1** "And you, bring near to you Aaron your brother, and his sons with him, from the midst of the Sons of Israel, to serve as priest to me: Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, the sons of Aaron. [fn1]

**28:2** And you shall make garments of holiness for Aaron your brother, for glory and for beauty. [fn2]

**28:3** And you shall speak to all the wise of heart whom I have filled with a spirit of wisdom, and they shall make the garments of Aaron to consecrate him, to serve as priest to me. [fn3]

**28:4** And these are the garments which they shall make: a breastpiece and an ephod and a robe and a tunic of checkered work and a turban and a sash; and they shall make garments of holiness for Aaron your brother and for his sons, to serve as priest to me. [fn4]

**28:5** And they shall take the gold and the blue and the purple and the scarlet yarn and the fine linen. [fn5]

---

## The Ephod (vv. 6-14)

**28:6** And they shall make the ephod of gold, blue and purple, scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, the work of a skilled craftsman. [fn6]

**28:7** Two shoulder pieces joined shall it have at its two ends, and it shall be joined. [fn7]

**28:8** And the skillfully woven band of his ephod which is upon it, according to its workmanship, shall be of one piece with it: gold, blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen. [fn8]

**28:9** And you shall take two onyx stones, and you shall engrave upon them the names of the Sons of Israel: [fn9]

**28:10** six of their names on the one stone, and the names of the six remaining on the second stone, according to their birth-order. [fn10]

**28:11** With the work of a stone engraver, like the engravings of a seal, you shall engrave the two stones with the names of the Sons of Israel; surrounded by settings of gold you shall make them. [fn11]

**28:12** And you shall set the two stones upon the shoulder pieces of the ephod, stones of remembrance for the Sons of Israel; and Aaron shall bear their names before YHWH upon his two shoulders as a remembrance. [fn12]

**28:13** And you shall make settings of gold; [fn13]

**28:14** and two chains of pure gold; twisted you shall make them, the work of a rope; and you shall put the chains of rope-work upon the settings. [fn14]

---

## The Breastpiece of Judgment (vv. 15-30)

**28:15** And you shall make a breastpiece of judgment, the work of a skilled craftsman; like the work of the ephod you shall make it: of gold, blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen you shall make it. [fn15]

**28:16** It shall be square, doubled over; a span its length and a span its width. [fn16]

**28:17** And you shall set in it a setting of stones, four rows of stones: a row of ruby, topaz, and emerald -- the first row; [fn17]

**28:18** and the second row: turquoise, sapphire, and diamond; [fn18]

**28:19** and the third row: jacinth, agate, and amethyst; [fn19]

**28:20** and the fourth row: beryl, onyx, and jasper. They shall be set in gold in their settings. [fn20]

**28:21** And the stones shall be according to the names of the Sons of Israel, twelve, according to their names; like the engravings of a seal, each according to his name they shall be, for the twelve tribes. [fn21]

**28:22** And you shall make upon the breastpiece chains of twisted work, the work of rope, of pure gold. [fn22]

**28:23** And you shall make upon the breastpiece two rings of gold, and you shall put the two rings upon the two ends of the breastpiece. [fn23]

**28:24** And you shall put the two cords of gold upon the two rings at the ends of the breastpiece. [fn24]

**28:25** And the two ends of the two cords you shall put upon the two settings, and you shall put them upon the shoulder pieces of the ephod, on its front. [fn25]

**28:26** And you shall make two rings of gold, and you shall set them upon the two ends of the breastpiece, upon its edge which is toward the ephod, inward. [fn26]

**28:27** And you shall make two rings of gold, and you shall put them upon the two shoulder pieces of the ephod beneath, on its front, close to its joining, above the skillfully woven band of the ephod. [fn27]

**28:28** And they shall bind the breastpiece by its rings to the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue, to be upon the skillfully woven band of the ephod; and the breastpiece shall not come loose from the ephod. [fn28]

**28:29** And Aaron shall bear the names of the Sons of Israel on the breastpiece of judgment upon his heart when he goes in to the holy place, as a remembrance before YHWH continually. [fn29]

**28:30** And you shall put into the breastpiece of judgment the Urim and the Thummim; and they shall be upon the heart of Aaron when he goes in before YHWH; and Aaron shall bear the judgment of the Sons of Israel upon his heart before YHWH continually. [fn30]

---

## The Robe of the Ephod (vv. 31-35)

**28:31** And you shall make the robe of the ephod entirely of blue. [fn31]

**28:32** And the opening for his head shall be in the midst of it; a binding shall be for its opening all around, the work of a weaver; like the opening of a coat of mail it shall be for it, so that it will not be torn. [fn32]

**28:33** And you shall make upon its hem pomegranates of blue and purple and scarlet yarn upon its hem all around, and bells of gold in the midst of them all around: [fn33]

**28:34** a bell of gold and a pomegranate, a bell of gold and a pomegranate, upon the hem of the robe all around. [fn34]

**28:35** And it shall be upon Aaron to minister; and its sound shall be heard when he goes in to the holy place before YHWH and when he goes out, so that he will not die. [fn35]

---

## The Gold Plate -- "Holy to YHWH" (vv. 36-38)

**28:36** And you shall make a plate of pure gold, and you shall engrave upon it the engravings of a seal: "Holy to YHWH." [fn36]

**28:37** And you shall put it on a cord of blue, and it shall be on the turban; on the front of the turban it shall be. [fn37]

**28:38** And it shall be upon the forehead of Aaron, and Aaron shall bear the iniquity of the holy things which the Sons of Israel shall consecrate, for all their holy gifts; and it shall be upon his forehead continually, for acceptance for them before YHWH. [fn38]

---

## The Tunic, Turban, and Sash (v. 39)

**28:39** And you shall weave the tunic of fine linen, and you shall make a turban of fine linen, and a sash you shall make, the work of an embroiderer. [fn39]

---

## Garments for Aaron's Sons (vv. 40-41)

**28:40** And for the sons of Aaron you shall make tunics, and you shall make for them sashes, and headdresses you shall make for them, for glory and for beauty. [fn40]

**28:41** And you shall clothe them -- Aaron your brother and his sons with him -- and you shall anoint them and you shall fill their hand and you shall consecrate them; and they shall serve as priests to me. [fn41]

---

## The Linen Undergarments (vv. 42-43)

**28:42** And you shall make for them undergarments of linen to cover the flesh of nakedness; from the hips and unto the thighs they shall be. [fn42]

**28:43** And they shall be upon Aaron and upon his sons when they go in to the tent of meeting, or when they draw near to the altar to minister in the holy place; and they shall not bear iniquity and die -- a statute forever for him and for his seed after him. [fn43]

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 28 transitions from sacred space to sacred person. Having completed the tabernacle and its furnishings (chapters 25-27), the text now addresses who may serve within them. The chapter's organizing principle is vestiture -- clothing as theology. The garments are described from outermost and most complex (ephod, breastpiece) to innermost and simplest (linen undergarments). The six garments listed in verse 4 -- breastpiece, ephod, robe, tunic, turban, sash -- are then described in an order reflecting their layering from outside to inside. The chapter's recurring emphasis on "bearing" (*nasa*) is theologically central: the high priest bears Israel's names on his shoulders (v. 12), bears their names over his heart (v. 29), bears the judgment of Israel before YHWH (v. 30), and bears the iniquity of the holy things on his forehead (v. 38). The dual purpose -- *lekhavod uletif'aret* ("for glory and for beauty," vv. 2, 40) -- frames the entire priestly wardrobe, establishing that worship is both an aesthetic act and a moral one.

### Key Decisions

**"Garments of holiness" for *bigdei qodesh* (vv. 2, 4):** EMET renders "garments of holiness." LOGOS renders "garments of holiness." PNEUMA renders "sacred garments." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "garments of holiness" preserves the Hebrew construct chain, where *qodesh* functions as a genitive of quality: these are garments whose essence is holiness, not merely garments that happen to be holy.

**"For glory and for beauty" for *lekhavod uletif'aret* (vv. 2, 40):** All three translators agree: "for glory and for beauty." This phrase is one of the most theologically concentrated in the chapter. *Kavod* ("weight, glory, honor") is the word used for YHWH's manifest presence; *tif'eret* ("beauty, splendor") designates aesthetic magnificence. The garments carry the weight of divine glory and the radiance of divine beauty.

**"Wise of heart" for *khakhmei lev* (v. 3):** EMET renders "wise of heart." LOGOS renders "wise of heart." PNEUMA renders "skilled workers." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "wise of heart" preserves the distinctive Hebrew localization of wisdom in the heart (*lev*), not the mind. PNEUMA's "skilled workers" communicates the practical sense but loses the theological register of Hebrew wisdom language.

**"Serve as priest" for *lekhahano* (vv. 1, 3, 4, 41):** EMET renders "to be a priest." LOGOS renders "to minister as priest." PNEUMA renders "to serve me as priest." The consensus adopts "to serve as priest" -- capturing both the piel denominative force of *kahan* ("to act as a kohen") and the relational direction of *li* ("to me"), while maintaining natural English.

**"Ruby" for *odem* (v. 17):** EMET renders "ruby." LOGOS renders "carnelian." PNEUMA renders "carnelian." The consensus follows EMET -- "ruby" for *odem* (from *adam*, "to be red"), since the Hebrew root points to a red stone. The identification of all twelve stones remains debated; see the Gemological Note below. This is a minority decision acknowledging that "carnelian" is equally defensible.

**"Headdresses" for *migba'ot* (v. 40):** EMET renders "headdresses." LOGOS renders "caps." PNEUMA renders "headbands." The consensus follows EMET -- "headdresses" for *migba'ot* (from *gava*, "to be high, rounded") preserves the formal dignity of the term and distinguishes these from Aaron's *mitsnefet* ("turban"), while remaining noncommittal about the exact shape.

**"Fill their hand" for *milleita et-yadam* (v. 41):** EMET renders "fill their hand." LOGOS renders "ordain them." PNEUMA renders "ordain them." The consensus follows EMET -- "fill their hand" preserves the concrete Hebrew idiom for priestly ordination (*mille yad*, literally "to fill the hand"), which derives from placing the inaugural offering in the priest's hands (cf. Lev 8:25-28). The metaphor communicates that priestly authority is received, not self-generated. LOGOS and PNEUMA's "ordain" is accurate but loses the distinctive Hebrew image.

**"Undergarments" for *mikhnesei* (v. 42):** EMET renders "undergarments." LOGOS renders "breeches." PNEUMA renders "undergarments." The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA -- "undergarments" for *mikhnesei* (from *kanas*, "to gather") avoids the archaic connotation of "breeches" while accurately conveying the garment's function and position as the innermost layer.

**"Flesh of nakedness" for *besar ervah* (v. 42):** EMET renders "flesh of nakedness." LOGOS renders "flesh of nakedness." PNEUMA renders "naked flesh." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "flesh of nakedness" preserves the construct chain and the theological weight of *ervah* ("nakedness, exposure, shame"), the same term used in the laws of Leviticus 18.

**"Statute forever" for *khuqqat olam* (v. 43):** Consistent with the decision at 27:21. EMET renders "a statute of perpetuity." LOGOS renders "a statute forever." PNEUMA renders "a lasting ordinance." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "a statute forever" is the most natural English rendering of the Hebrew construct chain. EMET's "of perpetuity" is unnecessarily formal; PNEUMA's "lasting ordinance" replaces *khuqqah* ("statute, inscribed decree") with the weaker "ordinance."

### Gemological Note

The identification of the twelve precious stones (vv. 17-20) is among the most debated questions in biblical scholarship. Many of the Hebrew stone names are rare or unique, and ancient gemological terminology does not map neatly onto modern classifications. The consensus renderings -- ruby, topaz, emerald; turquoise, sapphire, diamond; jacinth, agate, amethyst; beryl, onyx, jasper -- follow the traditional English identifications while acknowledging that nearly every term admits alternatives: *odem* may be carnelian rather than ruby; *sappir* is likely lapis lazuli rather than modern sapphire; *yahalom* may be onyx rather than diamond; *tarshish* may be chrysolite rather than beryl. The LXX stone names diverge significantly from later traditional identifications. What is theologically certain is the function: each of the twelve tribes is represented by a unique, precious stone, each engraved like a seal with a tribal name, all mounted in a single breastpiece worn over the priest's heart.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 28 (pp. 70-71) presents the LXX rendering with several notable features and divergences from the MT: (1) The LXX extensively reorders the chapter, particularly the breastpiece section, interleaving and repositioning material -- possibly reflecting a different Hebrew Vorlage or the translator's attempt to present the garments in their actual order of dressing. (2) The LXX renders Urim and Thummim as *delosis kai aletheia* ("manifestation and truth"), the chapter's most theologically significant interpretive rendering, associating the Urim with divine revelation and the Thummim with divine truth. (3) The LXX renders *tsits* (v. 36) as *petalon* ("petal, leaf, thin plate"), emphasizing the metallic form rather than the botanical metaphor. (4) The LXX translates *qodesh laYHWH* as *hagiasma kuriou* ("holiness of the Lord"), substituting *kurios* for the Tetragrammaton. (5) The gemstone identifications diverge in several positions (see Gemological Note). (6) The LXX renders *ruach chokhmah* (v. 3) as *pneuma aistheseos* ("spirit of perception"), an interpretive choice emphasizing aesthetic and practical discernment over abstract wisdom. (7) The LXX renders *milleita et-yadam* (v. 41) as *teleioseis tas cheiras auton* ("you shall perfect their hands"), using *teleioo* with its connotations of completion and perfection. (8) At v. 1, the LXX names the four sons consistently with the MT: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ve-attah haqrev eleikha et-Aharon akhikha ve-et-banav itto mi-tokh benei Yisrael lekhahano-li Aharon Nadav va-Avihu El'azar ve-Itamar benei Aharon*: "And you, bring near to you Aaron your brother, and his sons with him, from the midst of the Sons of Israel, to serve as priest to me: Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, the sons of Aaron"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Haqrev* (hiphil imperative of *qarav*, "to draw near") -- "cause to draw near"; the priestly role is defined spatially: priests are those drawn near to YHWH. The same root produces *qorban* ("offering"), linking the priest's calling to sacrificial presentation. *Lekhahano-li* (piel infinitive construct of *kahan* + 3ms suffix + *li*) -- "to serve as priest to me." EMET renders "to be a priest to me"; LOGOS renders "to minister as priest to Me"; PNEUMA renders "so that he may serve me as priest." The consensus adopts "to serve as priest to me" (see Key Decisions). The LXX uses *prosagagou* ("bring toward yourself"), preserving the spatial metaphor.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Ve-asita vigdei-qodesh le-Aharon akhikha lekhavod uletif'aret*: "And you shall make garments of holiness for Aaron your brother, for glory and for beauty"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Vigdei-qodesh* ("garments of holiness") -- PNEUMA renders "sacred garments"; the consensus preserves the construct chain (see Key Decisions). *Lekhavod uletif'aret* ("for glory and for beauty") -- *kavod* ("weight, glory, honor") is the word for YHWH's manifest presence (cf. 40:34); *tif'aret* ("beauty, splendor") denotes aesthetic magnificence. The priestly garments are theology made visible -- they carry the weight of divine glory and the radiance of divine beauty. The LXX renders as *eis timen kai doxan* ("for honor and glory").

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Ve-attah tedabber el-kol-khakhmei-lev asher milletiv ruakh khokhmah ve-asu et-bigdei Aharon leqadesho lekhahano-li*: "And you shall speak to all the wise of heart whom I have filled with a spirit of wisdom, and they shall make the garments of Aaron to consecrate him, to serve as priest to me"** -- EMET and LOGOS render "wise of heart"; PNEUMA renders "skilled workers." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS (see Key Decisions). *Milletiv* (piel perfect 1cs of *male* + 3ms suffix) -- "I have filled him"; God is the source of the artisan's skill. *Ruakh khokhmah* ("spirit of wisdom") -- the same divine endowment later bestowed explicitly upon Bezalel (31:3). The LXX renders *ruakh khokhmah* as *pneuma aistheseos* ("spirit of perception"), emphasizing aesthetic discernment.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Ve-elleh ha-begadim asher ya'asu khoshen ve-efod u-me'il u-khetonet tashbets mitsnefet ve-avnet*: "And these are the garments which they shall make: a breastpiece and an ephod and a robe and a tunic of checkered work and a turban and a sash"** -- All three translators agree in substance. The six garments are listed: *khoshen* ("breastpiece"), *efod* ("ephod"), *me'il* ("robe"), *khetonet tashbets* ("tunic of checkered work"), *mitsnefet* ("turban"), *avnet* ("sash"). PNEUMA renders *khetonet tashbets* as "woven tunic," which is adequate but less specific than "tunic of checkered work" -- *tashbets* (from *shavats*, "to weave in checker pattern") denotes a specific textile technique. The LXX lists the garments in a different order and renders *khoshen* as *peristethion* ("chest-piece").

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Ve-hem yiqkhu et-ha-zahav ve-et-ha-tekhelet ve-et-ha-argaman ve-et-tola'at ha-shani ve-et-ha-shesh*: "And they shall take the gold and the blue and the purple and the scarlet yarn and the fine linen"** -- All three translators agree. Five materials are listed -- gold is added to the four textile materials used previously (26:1, 31, 36; 27:16), since gold thread is woven into the ephod and breastpiece.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Ve-asu et-ha-efod zahav tekhelet ve-argaman tola'at shani ve-shesh moshzar ma'aseh khoshev*: "And they shall make the ephod of gold, blue and purple, scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, the work of a skilled craftsman"** -- All three translators agree. *Ma'aseh khoshev* ("work of a skilled craftsman/designer") -- from *khashav* ("to think, plan, devise"); the highest grade of textile work, also used for the *parokhet* (26:31). *Moshzar* ("twisted") -- the linen threads are twisted for strength and texture. The LXX renders *efod* as *epomis* ("shoulder-garment").

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Shtei khetafot khoverot yihyeh-lo el-shnei qetsotav ve-khubar*: "Two shoulder pieces joined shall it have at its two ends, and it shall be joined"** -- All three translators agree. *Khetafot* ("shoulder pieces") -- from *katef* ("shoulder"); the ephod has two straps over the shoulders. *Khoverot* (qal active participle feminine plural of *khavar*, "to join") -- "joining, attached." *Ve-khubar* (pual perfect) -- "and it shall be joined"; the shoulder pieces are integral to the ephod.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ve-kheshev afudato asher alav ke-ma'asehu mimmenu yihyeh zahav tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani ve-shesh moshzar*: "And the skillfully woven band of his ephod which is upon it, according to its workmanship, shall be of one piece with it"** -- EMET renders "the band of his ephod." LOGOS renders "the skillfully woven band of his ephod." PNEUMA renders "the skillfully woven waistband." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "skillfully woven band" for *kheshev* (related to *khashav*, "to devise") captures both the craft and function. *Mimmenu yihyeh* ("from it it shall be") -- the band is one piece with the ephod, not a separate attachment, continuing the pattern of organic unity seen in the cherubim and the lampstand.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Ve-laqakhta shtei avnei-shoham u-pittakhta aleihem shemot benei Yisrael*: "And you shall take two onyx stones, and you shall engrave upon them the names of the Sons of Israel"** -- All three translators agree. *Shoham* ("onyx") -- the exact identification is debated; possibly lapis lazuli or carnelian. *Pittakhta* (piel perfect of *patakh*) -- "you shall engrave"; engraving as "opening" the surface of the stone.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Shishah mi-shemotam al ha-even ha-ekhat ve-et-shemot ha-shishah ha-notarim al ha-even ha-shenit ke-toledotam*: "Six of their names on the one stone, and the names of the six remaining on the second stone, according to their birth-order"** -- EMET renders "birth-order." LOGOS renders "births." PNEUMA renders "the order of their birth." The consensus follows EMET -- "birth-order" for *ke-toledotam* (from *yalad*, "to bear") is the most precise single rendering.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Ma'aseh kharash even pittuhei khotam tefattakh et-shtei ha-avanim al-shemot benei Yisrael musabbot mishbetsot zahav ta'aseh otam*: "With the work of a stone engraver, like the engravings of a seal, you shall engrave the two stones with the names of the Sons of Israel; surrounded by settings of gold you shall make them"** -- All three translators agree. *Pittuhei khotam* ("engravings of a seal") -- the same engraving quality used for signet rings; the names are permanently, officially inscribed. *Mishbetsot* ("settings, filigree") -- from *shavats* ("to weave, set"); gold settings surround the stones.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ve-samta et-shtei ha-avanim al kitefot ha-efod avnei zikkaron li-vnei Yisrael ve-nasa Aharon et-shemotam lifnei YHWH al-shtei khetefav le-zikkaron*: "And you shall set the two stones upon the shoulder pieces of the ephod, stones of remembrance for the Sons of Israel; and Aaron shall bear their names before YHWH upon his two shoulders as a remembrance"** -- All three translators agree. *Zikkaron* ("remembrance, memorial") -- from *zakhar* ("to remember"); the stones are a physical prompt for divine remembrance. *Ve-nasa* ("and he shall bear") -- the first occurrence of the chapter's key verb: the priest carries the nation on his shoulders, the place of burden-bearing and strength.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Ve-asita mishbetsot zahav*: "And you shall make settings of gold"** -- All three translators agree. Gold filigree settings for mounting the onyx stones.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *U-shtei sharshrot zahav tahor migbalot ta'aseh otam ma'aseh avot ve-natattah et-sharshrot ha-avotot al-ha-mishbetsot*: "And two chains of pure gold; twisted you shall make them, the work of a rope; and you shall put the chains of rope-work upon the settings"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Sharshrot* ("chains") -- from the reduplicated root suggesting linked segments. *Migbalot* ("twisted, braided") -- from *gaval* ("to twist, border"). *Avot* ("rope, cord") -- the chains are braided gold cords. PNEUMA simplifies to "braided like cords"; the consensus preserves the Hebrew's technical description.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Ve-asita khoshen mishpat ma'aseh khoshev ke-ma'aseh efod ta'asenu zahav tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani ve-shesh moshzar ta'aseh oto*: "And you shall make a breastpiece of judgment, the work of a skilled craftsman; like the work of the ephod you shall make it"** -- All three translators agree. *Khoshen mishpat* ("breastpiece of judgment") -- *mishpat* ("judgment, decision, verdict") from *shafat* ("to judge"); this breastpiece contains the Urim and Thummim, instruments of divine decision-making. PNEUMA renders "breastpiece for making decisions," which captures the function but replaces the concrete noun *mishpat* with a verbal phrase. The LXX renders *khoshen* as *logeion* ("oracle, place of words"), interpreting function rather than form.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Ravua yihyeh kaful zeret orkho ve-zeret rokhbo*: "It shall be square, doubled over; a span its length and a span its width"** -- All three translators agree. *Kaful* ("doubled, folded double") -- from *kafal* ("to double, fold"); the breastpiece is folded to create a pouch for the Urim and Thummim. *Zeret* ("span") -- the distance from thumb-tip to little-finger tip, approximately 9 inches (half a cubit).

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *U-milleita vo millu'at even arba'ah turim even tur odem pitdah u-vareket ha-tur ha-rishon*: "And you shall set in it a setting of stones, four rows of stones: a row of ruby, topaz, and emerald -- the first row"** -- EMET renders "ruby." LOGOS renders "carnelian." PNEUMA renders "carnelian." The consensus follows EMET (see Key Decisions and Gemological Note). *Odem* (from *adam*, "to be red") -- a red stone; the Hebrew root favors a deeply red stone. *Pitdah* ("topaz") and *vareket* ("emerald," from *baraq*, "to flash, gleam") -- traditional identifications.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Ve-ha-tur ha-sheni nofekh sappir ve-yahalom*: "And the second row: turquoise, sapphire, and diamond"** -- All three translators agree. *Nofekh* ("turquoise" or "garnet"). *Sappir* ("sapphire," likely the ancient lapis lazuli rather than modern sapphire). *Yahalom* ("diamond," from *halam*, "to strike").

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Ve-ha-tur ha-shelishi leshem shevo ve-akhlamah*: "And the third row: jacinth, agate, and amethyst"** -- All three translators agree. *Leshem* ("jacinth" or "ligure"). *Shevo* ("agate"). *Akhlamah* ("amethyst," possibly from *khalam*, "to dream"; ancient tradition associated amethyst with dreams).

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Ve-ha-tur ha-revi'i tarshish ve-shoham ve-yashfeh meshubatsim zahav yihyu be-millu'otam*: "And the fourth row: beryl, onyx, and jasper. They shall be set in gold in their settings"** -- All three translators agree. *Tarshish* ("beryl" or "chrysolite," perhaps named for the region of Tarshish). *Shoham* ("onyx"). *Yashfeh* ("jasper"). *Meshubatsim* (pual participle of *shavats*) -- "set in filigree."

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ve-ha-avanim tihyeynah al-shemot benei-Yisrael shteim esreh al-shemotam pittuhei khotam ish al-shemo tihyeynah li-shnei asar shavet*: "And the stones shall be according to the names of the Sons of Israel, twelve, according to their names; like the engravings of a seal, each according to his name they shall be, for the twelve tribes"** -- All three translators agree. *Ish al-shemo* ("each man according to his name") -- *ish* is distributive here, "each one." Each stone bears one tribal name, making the breastpiece a miniature embodiment of all Israel over the priest's heart.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Ve-asita al-ha-khoshen sharshot gablut ma'aseh avot zahav tahor*: "And you shall make upon the breastpiece chains of twisted work, the work of rope, of pure gold"** -- All three translators agree. *Gablut* ("twisted, braided") -- from *gaval* ("to twist"); the same braided gold chains described in v. 14.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Ve-asita al-ha-khoshen shtei tabb'ot zahav ve-natattah et-shtei ha-tabb'ot al-shnei qetsot ha-khoshen*: "And you shall make upon the breastpiece two rings of gold, and you shall put the two rings upon the two ends of the breastpiece"** -- All three translators agree. Two rings at the upper corners of the breastpiece.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Ve-natattah shtei avotot ha-zahav al-shtei ha-tabb'ot el-qetsot ha-khoshen*: "And you shall put the two cords of gold upon the two rings at the ends of the breastpiece"** -- All three translators agree. The gold rope-chains connect the upper rings of the breastpiece to the ephod's shoulder pieces.

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Ve-et shtei qetsot shtei ha-avotot titten al-shtei ha-mishbetsot ve-natattah al-kitefot ha-efod el-mul panav*: "And the two ends of the two cords you shall put upon the two settings, and you shall put them upon the shoulder pieces of the ephod, on its front"** -- All three translators agree. *El-mul panav* ("to the front of its face") -- the chains attach at the front of the shoulder pieces.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Ve-asita shtei tabb'ot zahav ve-samta otam al-shnei qetsot ha-khoshen al-sefato asher el-ever ha-efod baytah*: "And you shall make two rings of gold, and you shall set them upon the two ends of the breastpiece, upon its edge which is toward the ephod, inward"** -- All three translators agree. *Baytah* ("inward, toward the inside") -- the lower rings are on the inner edge of the breastpiece, facing the ephod.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Ve-asita shtei tabb'ot zahav ve-natattah otam al-shtei kitefot ha-efod mi-lemattah mi-mul panav le-ummat mekhbarto mi-ma'al le-kheshev ha-efod*: "And you shall make two rings of gold, and you shall put them upon the two shoulder pieces of the ephod beneath, on its front, close to its joining, above the skillfully woven band of the ephod"** -- All three translators agree. *Mekhbarto* ("its joining, its seam") -- from *khavar* ("to join"); the point where the shoulder piece meets the ephod body.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Ve-yirkesu et-ha-khoshen mi-tabb'otav el-tabb'ot ha-efod bi-fetil tekhelet lihyot al-kheshev ha-efod ve-lo-yizzakh ha-khoshen me-al ha-efod*: "And they shall bind the breastpiece by its rings to the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue, to be upon the skillfully woven band of the ephod; and the breastpiece shall not come loose from the ephod"** -- All three translators agree. *Yirkesu* (qal imperfect of *rakhas*, "to bind, tie") -- a rare verb. *Yizzakh* (niphal imperfect of *zakhakh*, "to move away, slip") -- "shall not slip away"; the breastpiece must remain firmly attached to the ephod.

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *Ve-nasa Aharon et-shemot benei-Yisrael be-khoshen ha-mishpat al-libbo be-vo'o el-ha-qodesh le-zikkaron lifnei-YHWH tamid*: "And Aaron shall bear the names of the Sons of Israel on the breastpiece of judgment upon his heart when he goes in to the holy place, as a remembrance before YHWH continually"** -- All three translators agree. *Al-libbo* ("upon his heart") -- the names on the shoulders (v. 12) carry the burden; the names on the heart carry the intimacy. The priest bears Israel in strength (shoulders) and affection (heart) before YHWH. *Tamid* ("continually") -- the remembrance is perpetual.

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Ve-natattah el-khoshen ha-mishpat et-ha-Urim ve-et-ha-Tummim ve-hayu al-lev Aharon be-vo'o lifnei YHWH ve-nasa Aharon et-mishpat benei-Yisrael al-libbo lifnei YHWH tamid*: "And you shall put into the breastpiece of judgment the Urim and the Thummim; and they shall be upon the heart of Aaron when he goes in before YHWH; and Aaron shall bear the judgment of the Sons of Israel upon his heart before YHWH continually"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Urim* ("lights") -- from *or* ("light"); the plural of "light." *Tummim* ("perfections, completions") -- from *tamam* ("to be complete, perfect"). The Urim and Thummim are objects placed within the folded breastpiece-pouch for determining YHWH's will (cf. Num 27:21; 1 Sam 28:6). Their exact nature is never described; the text says nothing about their manufacture -- they are simply "put into" the breastpiece. PNEUMA renders "the means of decision for the Sons of Israel" for *et-mishpat benei-Yisrael*, which interprets *mishpat* rather than translating it. The consensus preserves the literal "the judgment of the Sons of Israel." The LXX renders Urim and Thummim as *delosis kai aletheia* ("manifestation and truth").

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Ve-asita et-me'il ha-efod kelil tekhelet*: "And you shall make the robe of the ephod entirely of blue"** -- All three translators agree. *Kelil* ("entirely, wholly") -- from *kalal* ("to complete"); the robe is entirely blue (*tekhelet*), the color associated with heaven and divinity, unlike the multicolored ephod and breastpiece. The robe is worn beneath the ephod.

> **[fn32] v.32 -- *Ve-hayah fi-rosho be-tokho safah yihyeh le-fiv saviv ma'aseh oreg ke-fi takhara yihyeh-lo lo yiqqarea*: "And the opening for his head shall be in the midst of it; a binding shall be for its opening all around, the work of a weaver; like the opening of a coat of mail it shall be for it, so that it will not be torn"** -- All three translators agree. *Fi* ("mouth, opening") -- the head-hole of the robe. *Takhara* ("coat of mail, leather armor") -- a rare word; the reinforced neckline is compared to armor. *Lo yiqqarea* ("it shall not be torn") -- niphal imperfect of *qara* ("to tear"); the intact robe signifies the unbroken priestly office.

> **[fn33] v.33 -- *Ve-asita al-shulaiv rimmonei tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani al-shulaiv saviv u-fa'amonei zahav be-tokham saviv*: "And you shall make upon its hem pomegranates of blue and purple and scarlet yarn upon its hem all around, and bells of gold in the midst of them all around"** -- All three translators agree. *Rimmonim* ("pomegranates") -- woven fabric pomegranates in three colors (without gold), symbols of fruitfulness and abundance. *Pa'amonim* ("bells") -- from *pa'am* ("to strike, beat"); small golden bells interspersed among the pomegranates.

> **[fn34] v.34 -- *Pa'amon zahav ve-rimmon pa'amon zahav ve-rimmon al-shulei ha-me'il saviv*: "A bell of gold and a pomegranate, a bell of gold and a pomegranate, upon the hem of the robe all around"** -- All three translators agree. The alternating pattern: bell -- pomegranate -- bell -- pomegranate, around the entire hem, creating an audible-visual rhythm of announcement and fruitfulness.

> **[fn35] v.35 -- *Ve-hayah al-Aharon le-sharet ve-nishma qolo be-vo'o el-ha-qodesh lifnei YHWH u-ve-tseto ve-lo yamut*: "And it shall be upon Aaron to minister; and its sound shall be heard when he goes in to the holy place before YHWH and when he goes out, so that he will not die"** -- All three translators agree. *Le-sharet* (piel infinitive construct of *sharat*, "to minister, serve") -- priestly service. *Ve-nishma qolo* ("and its sound shall be heard") -- the bells announce the priest's movements. *Ve-lo yamut* ("and he shall not die") -- the sound functions as a protective mechanism; an unannounced entry into the holy place brings death.

> **[fn36] v.36 -- *Ve-asita tsits zahav tahor u-pittakhta alav pittuhei khotam qodesh la-YHWH*: "And you shall make a plate of pure gold, and you shall engrave upon it the engravings of a seal: 'Holy to YHWH'"** -- All three translators agree. *Tsits* ("plate, blossom, flower") -- from *tsuts* ("to blossom, shine"); the word evokes both shining metal and a blossoming flower. *Qodesh la-YHWH* ("Holy to YHWH") -- the inscription on the high priest's forehead declares his consecration to the divine name. The LXX renders *tsits* as *petalon* ("petal, thin plate") and *qodesh la-YHWH* as *hagiasma kuriou* ("holiness of the Lord").

> **[fn37] v.37 -- *Ve-samta oto al-petil tekhelet ve-hayah al-ha-mitsnefet el-mul penei ha-mitsnefet yihyeh*: "And you shall put it on a cord of blue, and it shall be on the turban; on the front of the turban it shall be"** -- All three translators agree. The gold plate is fastened to the turban by a blue cord, positioned on the front.

> **[fn38] v.38 -- *Ve-hayah al-metsakh Aharon ve-nasa Aharon et-avon ha-qodashim asher yaqdihu benei Yisrael le-khol mattenot qodsheihem ve-hayah al-mitsekho tamid le-ratson lahem lifnei YHWH*: "And it shall be upon the forehead of Aaron, and Aaron shall bear the iniquity of the holy things which the Sons of Israel shall consecrate, for all their holy gifts; and it shall be upon his forehead continually, for acceptance for them before YHWH"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Avon* ("iniquity, guilt") -- from *avah* ("to bend, twist, distort"); the priest absorbs the ritual imperfections of Israel's sacred offerings. *Le-ratson* ("for acceptance, for favor") -- from *ratsah* ("to be pleased, accept"); the gold plate secures YHWH's acceptance despite imperfections. The priest's forehead bears both YHWH's holiness (the inscription) and Israel's guilt (the bearing function) simultaneously. PNEUMA renders *avon ha-qodashim* as "the guilt connected with the sacred offerings," which is interpretive but accurate; the consensus preserves the more literal "the iniquity of the holy things."

> **[fn39] v.39 -- *Ve-shibatsta ha-khetonet shesh ve-asita mitsnefet shesh ve-avnet ta'aseh ma'aseh roqem*: "And you shall weave the tunic of fine linen, and you shall make a turban of fine linen, and a sash you shall make, the work of an embroiderer"** -- All three translators agree. *Ve-shibatsta* (piel perfect of *shavats*, "to weave in checker pattern") -- "you shall weave in checkered work." The tunic and turban are plain white linen (*shesh*) -- no gold, no colored thread. The sash is embroidered work (*ma'aseh roqem*), a lower grade than the skilled craftsman's work (*ma'aseh khoshev*) of the ephod and breastpiece.

> **[fn40] v.40 -- *Ve-li-vnei Aharon ta'aseh khuttonot ve-asita lahem avnetim u-migba'ot ta'aseh lahem lekhavod uletif'aret*: "And for the sons of Aaron you shall make tunics, and you shall make for them sashes, and headdresses you shall make for them, for glory and for beauty"** -- EMET renders "headdresses." LOGOS renders "caps." PNEUMA renders "headbands." The consensus follows EMET (see Key Decisions). *Migba'ot* (from *gava*, "to be high, rounded") -- distinct from Aaron's *mitsnefet* ("turban"); the ordinary priests wear a different type of head-covering. The same dual purpose -- *lekhavod uletif'aret* ("for glory and for beauty") -- applies to all priestly garments.

> **[fn41] v.41 -- *Ve-hilbashta otam et-Aharon akhikha ve-et-banav itto u-mashakhta otam u-milleita et-yadam ve-qidashta otam ve-khihanu li*: "And you shall clothe them -- Aaron your brother and his sons with him -- and you shall anoint them and you shall fill their hand and you shall consecrate them; and they shall serve as priests to me"** -- EMET renders "fill their hand." LOGOS renders "ordain them." PNEUMA renders "ordain them." The consensus follows EMET (see Key Decisions). *U-mashakhta* (qal perfect of *mashakh*, "to anoint") -- the root of *mashiakh* ("anointed one, messiah"). *Milleita et-yadam* ("you shall fill their hand") -- the Hebrew idiom for priestly ordination, likely referring to placing the inaugural offering in the priest's hands (cf. Lev 8:25-28). *Ve-qidashta* (piel of *qadash*, "to be holy") -- "you shall consecrate, set apart." The four verbs form a sequence: clothe, anoint, ordain, consecrate.

> **[fn42] v.42 -- *Va-aseh lahem mikhnesei-vad le-khassot besar ervah mi-motnayim ve-ad-yerekhaim yihyu*: "And you shall make for them undergarments of linen to cover the flesh of nakedness; from the hips and unto the thighs they shall be"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Mikhnesei* ("undergarments, breeches") -- from *kanas* ("to gather, collect"). *Vad* ("linen") -- specifically white linen; distinct from *shesh* ("fine linen"). *Ervah* ("nakedness, exposure") -- from *arah* ("to be naked"); priestly service requires the covering of nakedness. LOGOS renders "breeches," which is archaic; the consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA with "undergarments" (see Key Decisions).

> **[fn43] v.43 -- *Ve-hayu al-Aharon ve-al-banav be-vo'am el-ohel mo'ed o be-gishtam el-ha-mizbeakh le-sharet ba-qodesh ve-lo-yis'u avon va-metu khuqqat olam lo u-le-zar'o akharav*: "And they shall be upon Aaron and upon his sons when they go in to the tent of meeting, or when they draw near to the altar to minister in the holy place; and they shall not bear iniquity and die -- a statute forever for him and for his seed after him"** -- All three translators agree in substance. *Be-gishtam* (qal infinitive construct of *nagash*, "to draw near, approach") -- "when they draw near." *Ve-lo-yis'u avon va-metu* ("and they shall not bear iniquity and die") -- the undergarments are not optional; neglecting them incurs guilt and death. *Khuqqat olam* ("a statute forever") -- the same formula that closed 27:21, now closing this chapter as well. The LXX renders *khuqqat olam* as *nomimon aionion* ("eternal ordinance"); *kurios* for YHWH.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
